courses.description: Using language is one of the most creative and distinctive of all the activities that make us human. Perhaps we are most aware of this kind of creativity when we read imaginative literature, whether classic or contemporary; but we also experience it directly in our own everyday language use. This course aims to foster new understandings of the various ways in which language influences and shapes the experiences of life common to all humanity. Students learn to analyze and appreciate a wide range of literary, linguistic and cultural phenomena, including the works of Hong Kong authors/poets who express themselves through English. From a linguistic perspective, we examine the ways in which language is structured and used creatively for different purposes in both local and global contexts. From a literary perspective, we study not only the traditional genres of fiction, poetry, and drama, but also film, song, and life writing. From a cultural perspective, we investigate how different texts and media are part of how we construct the world and define our evolving sense of self and our common humanity.
courses.course_syllabus:
Unit I: Imagination, critical thinking and creative expression through language and literature
• Conceptions of creativity and critical thinking
• Imagination and intellectual curiosity
• Culture and the central role of language and literature in the creation of meaning
• Language, literature and the study of the humanities

Unit II. English in an era of globalization
• International, global English(es) and intercultural contact
• Globalization and the (re)shaping of literature
• Diversity in English language(s) and literature (local and global)
• Ethical, global citizenship and intercultural citizenship
• Historical, political, social, and cultural issues emerging from and embedded in world Englishes and literature
• Critical, cultural analysis and appreciation of local/global English and literature (a variety of genres)

Unit III. Identity, language, and literature
• The role of language in shaping personal, social, and cultural identities
• The expression of identity, ethics and values through English language(s) and literature
• Creatively expressing one’s sense of self through English (e.g. poems, short stories, life writing, drama)
• Articulating both local and global identities; hybrid selves
• Critical, cultural analysis of the representation of local/global identities in literature (a variety of genres)
• Personal response to language and literature

courses.subject: ENGE
courses.title: Communications for English Majors I
courses.description: This course provides you with the communication skills necessary to perform various academic and non-academic tasks in both online and offline contexts. It demonstrates how differences in audience, communicative purpose and medium shape the content and structure of various written and spoken genres. It also discusses different patterns of development such as narration, description, exemplification, and persuasion, and the importance of thesis statements and topic sentences in organizing ideas. To help you develop an awareness of the structural and stylistic differences of various genres, you will produce a personal reflective essay, a literary analysis paper, and a persuasive presentation, that will be presented in various print, face-to-face and digital formats, using both linguistic and multimodal resources. Through a process oriented approach, you will be producing outlines and drafts of these writing tasks, get feedback, and learn writing strategies such as using sensory details, choosing active, specific verbs, and employing cohesive devices. Recognizing how technology shapes communication practices in diverse ways, this course also draws attention to the affordances and constraints of digital tools such as WordPress, Blackboard, Zoom, and social media platforms.
courses.learning_outcomes:
By the end of this course, students should be able to:

1. Write a personal reflective essay that uses elements of description and narration
2. Construct a literary analysis paper that examines a short story
3. Conduct a brief presentation that persuades audiences to adopt a specific action
4. Recognize the structures and conventions of various spoken and written genres e.g. advertisements, social media, public talk

Understand how differences in audience, purpose, medium of communication shape the register, style, and organization of texts.
courses.course_syllabus:
To achieve the abovementioned learning outcomes, this course will cover the following major concepts and topics:

1. **Genres**: the short story as a literary genre; digital genres such as online ads, social media profiles; the public talk as a spoken genre
2. **Medium and context**:

* Identifying audience and purpose
* Understanding the affordances and constraints of different media
* Choosing appropriate register and style
* Using multimodal resources

1. **Content and structure**

* Recognizing patterns of development: narration, description, exemplification, persuasion
* Organizing ideas in an outline: introduction, body, conclusion
* Formulating thesis statements and topic sentences
* Building supporting evidence: textual evidence, concrete and specific examples

1. **Language focus**

* Using sensory details
* Choosing active, specific verbs
* Employing cohesive devices

1. **Tools**

* Blogging platforms (e.g. WordPress)
* Learning management systems (e.g. Blackboard)
* Online learning platforms (e.g. Zoom)
* Presentation software (e.g. PowerPoint)

1. **Citation style:** MLA
courses.feedback_evaluation: As with all courses in the English Department, students evaluate the course through a survey and written comments at the end of the term. In addition, discussion of class activities and their effectiveness is ongoing, given the ample opportunity during class for discussion of pedagogy and the range and effectiveness of the approaches students are taking in accomplishing the course goals.
courses.required_readings: This is no required textbook for this course. Assigned readings and worksheets will be made available through Blackboard.

courses.subject: ENGE
courses.title: Communications for English Majors II
courses.description: This course builds on the foundational communication skills introduced in ENGE 1310 and helps you develop the competencies necessary to conduct effective research. It examines the existing structures and conventions of various print and digital genres such as journal articles, blogs, infographics, and YouTube videos. It also discusses different patterns of development such as definition, problem/ solution, comparison/ contrast, classification and division, and the importance of maintaining an appropriate register and style. To help you produce and consume knowledge in creative and critical ways, you will construct an abstract, an annotated bibliography, a literature review, a video, and a research article that discusses an issue in applied linguistics. These texts will be constructed for real world audiences and will involve the use of various multimodal resources. Through a process-oriented approach, you will be producing outlines and drafts of these writing tasks, get feedback, and learn writing strategies such as signposting, using signal phrases, and constructing parallel structures. Recognizing the increasing importance of technology in producing and consuming knowledge, this course also discusses the use of various digital tools such as search engines, reference management software, blogging and video editing platforms.
courses.enrollment_requirement: Prerequisite: ENGE1310.
courses.learning_outcomes:
By the end of this course, students should be able to:

1. Write a literature review that provides an overview of existing research on a particular topic
2. Produce a research paper on a specific issue in applied linguistics
3. Compose a short video that uses multimodal resources to present a research topic
4. Recognize the structures and conventions of various spoken and written genres e.g. journal articles, books, infographics, educational videos
5. Understand how differences in audience, purpose, medium of communication shape the register, style, and organization of texts.
courses.course_syllabus:
To achieve the abovementioned learning outcomes, this course will cover the following major concepts and topics:

1. **Genres**: academic genres such as journal articles, monographs, and edited volumes; digital genres such as infographics, news websites, Wikipedia, blogs, and YouTube videos
2. **Medium and context**:

* Identifying audience and purpose
* Understanding the affordances and constraints of different media
* Choosing appropriate register and style
* Using multimodal resources

1. **Content and structure**

* Recognizing patterns of development: definition, problem/solution, comparison/contrast, classification and division
* Organizing ideas in an outline: introduction, body, conclusion
* Formulating thesis statements and topic sentences
* Building supporting evidence: textual evidence, concrete and specific examples

1. **Language focus**

* Signposting
* Using signal phrases
* Constructing parallel structures

1. **Tools**

courses.subject: ENGE
courses.title: Introduction to English Linguistics
courses.description: This course offers an overview of the linguistic structure of English. It systematically studies the English language at various levels: words, phrases, sentences and beyond. It also provides students with the opportunity to observe and explain the ways in which English is used in everyday life.
courses.learning_outcomes:
Upon completing this course, students should be able to:

♦ Observe the characteristics of modern English language

♦ Understand some basic concepts in linguistics

♦ Describe how words are formed in English (Morphology)

♦ Analyze the structure of English phrases and sentences (Syntax)

♦ Explain how meaning is organized in our mind (Semantics)

♦ Identify how language is used by speakers in actual social interaction (Pragmatics)

♦ Examine how sentences in spoken and written language form larger meaningful units of communication within contexts (Discourse Analysis)

♦ Understand how languages are acquired (Language acquisition)

♦ Observe how people use English in society (Sociolinguistics)
courses.course_syllabus:
This course offers a comprehensive overview of the English linguistics. We explore the characteristics and structure of the English language and human language at various levels: words, phrases, sentences and beyond. The course also offers students the opportunity to observe the ways in which English is used in everyday life. Some of the questions to be dealt with are:

♦ What is the nature of human language?

♦ What is linguistics?

♦ What is the difference between language learning and linguistics?

♦ How can we analyze the structure of English as a system of communication?

♦ What is the role of English in different domains in our society (e.g. in school, at home, on the Internet)?

courses.description: This course is designed for English Majors who need some knowledge of the grammatical structure of contemporary English, and for those who may want to study English linguistics and English teaching courses which require such background information. It covers basic questions such as what grammar is and how one goes about studying the structure of English. Basic grammatical concepts and categories as well as the constituent structure are systemically introduced to equip students with the essential analytical tools.
courses.learning_outcomes:
At the end of the course, you should be able to:

• recognise English grammatical structures

• describe those structures accurately and concisely

• write about and discuss issues relating to the grammar of English
courses.course_syllabus:
The course deals with contemporary English, that is, English as it is currently used in both writing and speech. Topics covered will include (subject to minor modification):

• Form & function in grammatical analysis

• Verb complementation patterns

• Adverbials

• Tense and aspect

• Modal Verbs

• Nouns and noun phrases

• Adjectives and adverbs

• Prepositions and prepositional phrases

• Coordination and subordination

• Information structure

courses.subject: ENGE
courses.title: Introduction to Literature
courses.description: This course will introduce students to methods of reading literary texts from the major genres of fiction, drama, and poetry. The emphasis will be on specific literary texts, paying attention to their language, forms, conventions and meanings.
courses.course_syllabus:
Introduction to literature

- definition of “literature”

- issue of “canon”

- reading literature

Fiction

- plot, theme

- character, point of view

- setting, style, symbol

- text and context

Poetry

- poetic meaning: speaker, subject/situation, tone

- poetic language: diction, syntax, imagery, figurative language

- poetic sound: rhythm, rhyme

- poetic form: stanza forms, sonnet, free verse

Drama

- dramatic elements

- stage properties

- speech

- tragedy and comedy

courses.subject: ENGE
courses.title: Superheroes in Comics and Film
courses.description:
(UGED2187 is double-coded with ENGE2140.)

What’s a hero? What’s a superhero? Why and when do we need superheroes? How to be one and who gets to be one? We will begin by tracing the origins of this enduring pop fiction, from the hero’s journey in antiquity to the outbreak of the Second World War. We will also explore the moral ambiguity and mixed values of some of the most memorable characters. Furthermore, we will examine revisionist approaches to the superhero genre that respond to the hopes and disillusionments of the contemporary era. Topics covered will include good and evil, power and identity, and justice and responsibility. You may also have the chance to design your own superheroes. It is hoped that by the end of the course, you will develop new ways of reading superhero narratives as uniquely modern myths.
courses.learning_outcomes:
Having successfully completed this course students shall be able to:

* comprehend the developments and significance of the superhero genre
* understand elements of effective storytelling
* analyse form, style, and technique in the interpretation of different mediums
* investigate, discuss and write critically, reflectively, and analytically

courses.subject: ENGE
courses.title: Gods Behaving Badly: Myths and Legends from Around the World
courses.description:
(UGED2197 is double-coded with ENGE2190.)

The course will consider myths and legends from around the world with particular sensitivity to one peculiar aspect of them: that the gods depicted in them often behave no better than the people whose lives they determine. In order to explain why this is the case, the course will seek to understand the nature and function of myth, and the complex uses to which they are put in order to explain some of our most pressing questions: How did we all begin? Where are we? Who are we? Why is there so much wrong with the world? What’s the solution? Where will we go when we die? How will it all end? Although the concept of myth might seem outdated in modern secular culture, by drawing comparisons between the ancient and contemporary worlds, students will be encouraged to see how myth-making of some kind is as inevitable and innately human as the questions which prompt it. The course will begin by establishing some archetypal similarities between myths – from myths of Creation to myths of Apocalypse – and then consider myths from specific cultures – including Greco-Roman, Scandinavian, and Chinese – before considering the appeal of myth in contemporary popular culture, especially The Lord of Rings, and Game of Thrones.
courses.learning_outcomes:
* Students will acquire knowledge of the different types of myths from ancient and contemporary worlds in order to comprehend the similarities and differences between them.

* Students will learn to analyse various myths as vehicles for cultural values and anxieties.

* Students will evaluate the power and persuasiveness of myths to give answers to presiding moral and existential questions including the meaning of life, the nature of death, and the existence of God.

* Students will synthesise their knowledge of a broad range of myths in order to understand the greater role of myth in human society.

* Students will critique the way contemporary technology has facilitated a new conception of mythology and myth-making.

* Students will evaluate the representation of gender in myth.
courses.course_syllabus:

| 2 | What is a God? | Gods as archetype, the Great Mother Gender roles and the gods Demi-gods and Godlike Humans |
| 3 | How did it all begin?: Creation Myths | Creation stories from Australian Aboriginal, Babylonian, Christian, Norse, and Japanese narratives. Reading: *Genesis* (selections); “Enuma Elish” (selections); Hesiod, *Theogony* (selections) |

courses.subject: ENGE
courses.title: Existentialism and Literature
courses.description:
(UGED2172 is double-coded with ENGE2220.)
This course looks at the close relationship between existentialist philosophy and literature. Existentialism is a philosophical movement that focuses on human existence and the challenges of choice, freedom, and responsibility in the modern world. We will explore a variety of existentialist positions—including those of Kierkegaard, Nietzsche, Heidegger, Sartre, and de Beauvoir—to understand how existentialist thought constitutes an important intellectual context for many themes in modern literature, including authenticity, alienation, and anxiety. Authors to be discussed include Kafka, Dostoevsky, Ionesco, Camus, Beckett and others. Students interested in literary representations of the existential challenges of the human condition are welcome to attend this course.
courses.learning_outcomes:
By the end of the course, students should be able to:

* analyze and synthesize some of the most distinct themes and concepts in the existentialist movement
* analyze and evaluate various approaches to reading existentialist themes in modern literature
* appreciate the importance, relevance, and value of existentialist themes in modern literature
* produce a well-reasoned critical essay
courses.course_syllabus:
* **Week 1**
+ Topic: Introduction (syllabus, reading material exam issues)
- Description: In this lecture, we will go through all the practical issues related to this course.

* **Week 2**
+ Topic: Introduction to Existentialism and Literature
- Description: This lecture provides a broad overview of the historical and intellectual background of existentalist literature, and some of the major ideas associated with this movement.

* **Week 3**
+ Topic: Choice and Responsibility
- Description: In Week 3, we will explore how Sartre’s notion of ‘choice’ plays a crucial role in modern literature, including *Nausea* and “Drive my Car.”

* **Week 4**
+ Topic: Freedom and Anxiety
- Description: This lecture explores the difference between ‘fear’ and ‘anxiety’, and how this difference is often thematized in modern literature and art.

* **Week 5**
+ Topic: The Ethics of Existence
- Description: This week, we explore the relationship between individual freedom and moral responsibility.

* **Week 6**
+ Topic: Alienation and Guilt
- Description: Guilt and alienation are some of the major themes in existentialist literature. This lecture explains how Kafka and Ionesco address these themes.

* **Week 7**
+ Topic: Authentic Living, Das Man, Subjects and Objects
- Description: What is meant by an ‘authentic’ life—as opposed to a life of gestures, illusions, and performances? We will explore this question through a discussion of Heidegger’s philosophy and the film *The Truman Show.*

* **Week 8**
+ Topic: The Absurd and the Meaning of Life
- Description: One of the best known existentalist writers, Albert Camus introduced the philosophy of the absurd. This lecture provides an introduction to this philosophy through a discussion of the novel *The Stranger.*

* **Week 9**
+ Topic: Repetition and the Eternal Recurrence of the Same
- Description: Nietzsche formulates a way of thinking about the repetitiveness of existence, and this lecture will apply these thoughts in connection with a discussion of the film *Groundhog Day.*

* **Week 10**
+ Topic: Language and Presence
- Description: This lecture focuses on the role of language in existentialist literature, and more specifically the gap between language and reality.
* **Week 11**
+ Topic: Waiting, Inaction, Boredom
- Description: Accompanying the sense of individual freedom, protagonists in existentialist literature often experience a sense of purposelessness. The lecture will discuss this challenge in connection with works by Beckett and Coppola.
* **Week 12**
+ Topic: Memory, Regret, and Existence

courses.subject: ENGE
courses.title: Children's Literature
courses.description:
(UGED2194 is double-coded with ENGE2360.)

This course will provide an outline of developments in children’s literature in England and parts of Europe through the study of some essential, central texts as well as recent books for children. The uses of fantasy and the educational aspects of books for children will be discussed, along with notions of childhood and the nature of children. Through close reading of set texts students will be able to engage in critical techniques applicable to most literature, for the best texts for children satisfy sensitive adult readers too.
courses.learning_outcomes:
1. To analyse the pedagogical and cultural values of children’s literature.

2. To evaluate the techniques employed in writing children’s literature and in representations of children.

3. To explore the social, historical and political background of the themes and motifs in children’s literature.

4. To critically evaluate the ideas and values embedded in children’s literature.
courses.course_syllabus:
Introduction: “Childhood is the Kingdom where Nobody Dies” - We will look at childhood as an inner affective condition rather than a biological condition.

Approaches to Reading Fairy Tales: Grimms’ Fairy Tales / Andersen‘s Fairy Tales – We will explore themes such as good vs evil and gender representation in fairy tales.

Romantic Notions of Childhood: Songs of Innocence and Experience – We will analyse how the poems convey social critique.

Representations of Childhood in Victorian Literature: [Extracts provided] – We will evaluate Victorian notions of class and how this influences the representations of children in literature.

A Child’s Journey: Alice’s Adventures in Wonderland / Peter Pan – We will analyse elements of fantasy in children’s literature.

Children as Heroic Figures: Harry Potter and the Philosopher’s Stone – We will analyse how children are represented as heroes in children’s literature.

Illusion vs Reality: Harry Potter and the Curious Incident – We will explore how modern anxieties are expressed in children’s literature.

Narrating the Real World: The Curious Incident of the Dog in the Night-time – We analyse the genre of realism in children’s literature.

courses.subject: ENGE
courses.title: Twentieth-Century Fiction
courses.description: This course introduces students to the distinctly modern features found in the development of the western novel in the twentieth century. Emphasis will be put on narrative representation as a way of capturing a consciousness specific to the modern age. Novelists to be studied will normally include: Conrad, Joyce, Woolf, Lessing, Lawrence, Beckett and others.
courses.learning_outcomes:
Understanding 20th Century fiction in terms of historical and social contexts. Students will be able to show familiarity with the social, historical, and biographical backgrounds of each text/author covered in this course.
Identifying some of the major themes and concerns of 20th Century fiction. Students will be able to find a set of thematic ideas that have emerged from their reading of the texts in the course.
Grasping the development of the genre of fiction. Students will be able to describe the distinctive features of 20th Century fiction in terms of style and narrative technique.
Applying critical concepts to the study of 20th Century fiction. Students will be able to employ the key terms and concepts of reader-response, ideological, feminist, psychoanalytic and postcolonial criticism in their analyses.
courses.course_syllabus:
The aesthetic value and ideological functions to fiction in 20th Century: To study how fiction as a formal, aesthetic construct functions in social discourse and in response to historical changes.
Literary conventions and innovations: To study how fiction develops in 20th Century through stylistic and thematic revisions of the traditional novel.
Modernism and its major issues: To study how 20th Century fiction represents self, reality, psychic processes, and the question of language.
Literary criticism and interpretation: To study how 20th Century fiction means interpretively, emotionally and symbolically to various readers.
courses.feedback_evaluation: Students are welcome to make comments and feedback on the course; and students are encouraged to share their ideas with the instructor. At the end of each lecture, students will be given opportunity to raise questions; and in tutorials students are free to exchange views and opinions with the instructor and tutors. Students may also use email system to communicate their suggestions and requests.

courses.subject: ENGE
courses.title: Reading Poetry
courses.description: This course provides an introduction to the art of reading poetry. We will read and analyze a range of poems of diverse styles and forms from the Renaissance to the present day so as to stimulate students’ creative and critical responses to poetry. We will also read and analyze contemporary poetry written in Hong Kong, Malaysia and Singapore. The course explores, among other topics, the role of the imagination, the act of writing, the purposes of poetry, the notions of tradition and of the individual talent, for these are issues with which poets engage, whether directly or indirectly, in their poems.
courses.learning_outcomes:
1. To enjoy reading poetry and to understand the act of reading as a critical exercise.

2. To understand poetry as a form of creative violence inflicted on language.

3. To explore the social, historical and political background of the formation of themes and motifs in poetry.

4. To critically evaluate the ideas, values and effects embedded in poetry.

courses.subject: ENGE
courses.title: English Phonetics and Phonology
courses.description: This course introduces students to basic concepts in phonetics, the scientific study of speech, and in phonology, the study of sound patterns in various human languages with principal emphasis on the English language. Students gain an understanding of the articulatory mechanisms for producing consonants, vowels, as well as tone and intonation. The International Phonetic Alphabet and sounds from a variety of languages are introduced. In the phonology part of the course, students analyze the patterns governing the distribution of sounds in different languages. A major aim of this course is to enable students to transcribe English words and utterances and to develop an appreciation of the diversity and systematicity of sound structure in human language.
courses.course_syllabus: This course introduces students to basic concepts in phonetics, the scientific study of speech, and in phonology, the study of sound patterns in various human languages with principal emphasis on the English language. Students will gain an understanding of the articulatory mechanisms for producing consonants, vowels, as well as stress and intonation. They will be introduced to the International Phonetic Alphabet, and sounds from a variety of languages. In the phonology part of the course, students will analyze the patterns governing the distribution of sounds in different languages. A major aim of this course is to enable students to transcribe English words and utterances, and to develop an appreciation of the diversity and systematicity of sound structure in human language.
courses.feedback_evaluation: At the end of the course, students will be asked to complete Course Evaluations for both the lectures and tutorials for ENGE2510. This information is highly valued and is used to revise teaching methods, tasks, and content. During the term, students are also strongly encouraged to provide feedback on the course via email or meetings with the professor or tutors. After the midterm, students will also be asked to provide feedback via a brief questionnaire on the course content, teaching methods, and tasks in order to determine if adjustments need to be made for the remainder of the term.

courses.subject: ENGE
courses.title: Hong Kong English and its Culture
courses.description:
(UGEC2188 is double-coded with ENGE2530.)

The objective of this course is to give students an overview of the status, features, and use of Hong Kong English (HKE), the variety of English commonly used in Hong Kong. In the first part of the course, students will be introduced to the concept of ‘world Englishes’ and examine different theoretical frameworks for conceptualizing the evolution of new varieties of English. The course then examines relationship between Hong Kong English and Hong Kong culture from cultural, social, historical, and educational perspectives, particularly in relation to and juxtaposition from Cantonese and Putonghua. The second part of the course examines both spoken and written features of HKE, including grammar, discourse particles, vocabulary, and pronunciation, as well as the practices of code-mixing and code-switching. This section of the course also focuses on the social impact of the use of these features in Hong Kong culture. In the third part of the course, language attitudes and ideologies towards ‘standard’ language varieties (for example American and British English) in relation to HKE will be explored. The course will also examine the relationship between use of HKE and social identity as well as gender in Hong Kong culture.
courses.course_syllabus:
Week 1

• Introduction: syllabus, reading material, teaching method

The first week will introduce students to the course and the course syllabus.

• No reading

Week 2

• Topic: New varieties of English

In week 2, the ‘how’ of Hong Kong English will be discussed, and key frameworks to analyze the evolution of new Englishes will be examined.

* *•** Reading: Hansen Edwards 2018 Chapter 2

Week 3

• Topic: A social and linguistic history of English in Hong Kong

In week 3, the ‘how’ of Hong Kong English will continued to be examined through the presentation of a social, cultural, and linguistic history of Hong Kong, which emphasizes the history and status of English in Hong Kong society in juxtaposition to both Cantonese and Putonghua.

* *•** Reading: Hansen Edwards 2018 Chapter 1; Chan, 2002; Evans, 2017

Week 4

• Topic: What’s in a name? Chinglish, Kongish, and Hong Kong English

In week 4, the course will focus on the ‘what’ of Hong Kong English. It will examine and differentiate among three terms often used to describe the English of Hong Kong: Kongish, Chinglish, and Hong Kong English, particular in terms of the social meanings of these terms in Hong Kong culture. These terms will be explored against the frameworks discussed in week 2.

* *•** Reading: Bolton, 2002; Leung, 2015

Week 5

• Topic: Features of HKE – Pronunciation

Week 5 continues the focus on the ‘what’ of HKE through a discussion of the pronunciation features of HKE. This week also explores the social impact of use of these features in Hong Kong society.

* *•** Reading: Hansen Edwards 2018 Chapter 7; Deterding, Wong, and Kirkpatrick, 2008

Week 6

• Topic: Features of HKE – Grammar

Week 6 continues the examination of features of Hong Kong English by examining the grammar of HKE. This week also explores the social impact of use of these features in Hong Kong society.

* *•** Reading: Hansen Edwards 2018 Chapter 7; Sung, 2015

Week 7

• Topic: Features of HKE – Vocabulary

Week 7 focuses on the vocabulary (including discourse particles) of HKE. This week also explores the social impact of use of these features in Hong Kong society.

• Reading: Hansen Edwards 2018 Chapter 7; *New Hong Kong English Words (OED)*

Week 8

• Topic: Written and spoken HKE

In week 8, the ‘when’ of HKE will be explored, particularly in relation to spoken vs. written language as well as code-mixing and code-switching. This week also explores the social meaning of code-switching and code-mixing in Hong Kong culture.

* *•** Reading: Li, 2000; Sung, 2015

Week 9

• Topic: Attitudes towards HKE

In week 9, students will first be introduced to the concepts of ‘language attitudes’ and ‘standard language ideologies’. Attitudes towards HKE both locally and globally will then be examined, particularly in relation to ‘standard’ varieties of English such as American and British English. This essentially explores the ‘why’ of HKE.

•Reading: Hansen Edwards 2018 Chapters 2, 5, 6; Chan, 2016

Week 10

• Topic: HKE and social identity

In week 10, the ‘who’ of HKE will be explored as the discussion focuses on the use of HKE as a social identity marker.

* *•**Reading: Hansen Edwards 2018 Chapters 3 & 6

Week 11

• Topic: HKE and gender

Week 11 continues the exploration of the ‘who’ of HKE through the examination of gender and HKE.

* *•** Reading: Hansen Edwards 2018 Chapter 6

Week 12

• Topic: HKE in social media

In week 12, the use of Hong Kong English in social media, including on Facebook and YouTube and by artists such as GYOBN, will be explored. This discussion will also revisit the use of the terms Kongish and Chinglish, and their use by social media in relation to HKE.

* *•** Reading: Chan, 2015; Li, 2015; Yau, 2016

Week 12

• Topic: Conclusions and revisions

In the final week of the course, a revision of the course content will conducted and final conclusions will be drawn about Hong Kong English.

* *•** No reading

courses.subject: ENGE
courses.title: Forensic Linguistics: Language as Evidence in Legal Processes
courses.description: This course introduces the role linguistics and linguists play in forensic science and legal investigations. Forensic linguists are like the Sherlock Holmes of language – they offer their expert opinions about linguistic evidence in the legal process. This course introduces some major topics in forensic linguistics, including authorship analysis, speaker identification, trial discourse, plagiarism and collusion, and linguistic evidence in cybercrime. High profile cases where forensic linguists have contributed to legal investigations will be discussed. Students will also be introduced the methods and tools commonly used in forensic linguistic analyses of spoken and written data. No prior knowledge of law is required.
courses.course_syllabus:
Course Topics and Schedule

Lecture 1: What is Forensic Linguistics and the Role of the Forensic Linguist
This lecture defines the scope and nature of forensic linguistics as a sub-branch of applied linguistics. It also introduces the responsibilities of a forensic linguist in legal proceedings.

Lecture 2: Language of the Legal Process
This lecture outlines the characteristics of legal discourse style and register, as well as the language of police interviewing and testimonies.

Lecture 3: Discourse of the Courtroom
This lecture discusses the trial as a complex genre, the power of language in the courtroom, and the role of the expert witness in the courtroom.

Lecture 4: Types of Forensic Texts and Linguistic Evidence
This lecture identify the types of texts that can be used as forensic evidence, such as suicide notes, death threats, email and mobile texts.

Lecture 5: Analyzing Linguistic Evidence
Students will be introduced to the major tools and methods that can be used for analyzing linguistic evidence, such as transcription, stylistic analysis, discourse analysis, author profiling, etc.

Lecture 6 & Lecture 7 Authorship Attribution
This lecture introduces the process in which linguists conduct stylistic analysis to identify the author(s) of disputed or questioned texts, as well as the consistency and distinctiveness of an author’s linguistic style. Students will also be working on activities to conduct authorship analysis.

Lecture 8: Plagiarism and Collusion
This lecture deals with the concept of textual borrowing by looking into the issue of plagiarism in academic and legal contexts, as well as the problem of collusion in police statements.

Lecture 9: Forensic Phonetics
This lecture focuses on the use of spoken data as forensic evidence. It discusses the issues of transcribing and analyzing human voices, and how a speaker’s social background may be derived on the basis of their voice.

Lecture 10: Online Aggression and Freedom of Expression
This lecture critically examines the issue of hate speech and online aggression in light of related legal and social issues such as racism and discrimination.

Lecture 11: Linguistic Evidence in Cybercrime
This lecture discusses the growing trend of cybercrime on the internet and the role of forensic linguistics in cybercrime cases.

The rest of the class hours will be devoted to student presentations and a mid-term quiz.
courses.required_readings:
1. What is Forensic Linguistics and the Role of the Forensic Linguist
- Ch.1 in Coulthard et al. (2017); Ch. 1 in Olsson & Luchjenbroers (2018).

2. Language of the Legal Process
- Bhatia, V. K. (1987). Language of the law. Language teaching, 20(4), 227-234. Ch.4 in Coulthard et al. (2017)

3. Discourse of the Courtroom
- Heffer, C. (2010). Narrative in the trial. The Routledge handbook of forensic linguistics, Nueva York, Routledge, 199-217. Ch.5 in Coulthard et al. (2017)

4. Types of Forensic Texts and Linguistic Evidence

courses.description:
(UGEC2189 is double-coded with ENGE2600.)

Although English has long spread around the world, the consequences of this spread are only now beginning to be appreciated by speakers in diverse cultures and societies. English has changed the world, impacting specific local cultures in both positive and negative ways. But English has also been localized and acculturated, made to express the realities of diverse cultures and societies, and so the language has been changed in turn. The discourse of world Englishes aims to re-imagine our understanding of the language. The difference between error and innovation can no longer be decided by reference to ‘ownership’ of the language. Additionally, the language is beginning to be a medium of the expression of identity for more and more people in very different social and cultural contexts. World English must be pluralized, which is why we think in terms of world Englishes. This course is split into two sections. The first section will introduce you to issues in the discourse of world Englishes: innovation, variety, creativity, globalization, etc. The second section will look at specific case studies in different cultures and societies, and will focus on Asian Englishes, e.g. English in Singapore, China, and Japan. Different Asian cultures have distinct historical experiences with English, leading to different levels of cultural penetration (e.g. in popular culture) and depth of function (e.g. in business and law). The second section looks at different examples to explore their specific cultural engagements with the English language, but also to identify what they have in common: Japan English and Singlish develop in very different cultures, but the course with emphasis comparison as well as contrast.
courses.course_syllabus:
* introduces popular ideas and linguistic theories about World Englishes;

* contextualizes examples of World Englishes, especially in Asian cultures and societies;

* builds vocabulary and demonstrates different methods for study in linguistics and culture;

* develops a range of study skills relevant to further undergraduate studies in general.

The main topics and regions to be covered are as follows, along with required reading and issues raised:

Introduction: McWhorter, ‘The cosmopolitan tongue’, *World Affairs Journal* Vol. 172, No. 2 (2009), pp.61-68.

In this week the course will raise general questions about the utility and desirability of a global language. Many have celebrated English as a global language, and the course will consider both positive and negative possibilities that will be investigated using specific examples later in the term.

Histories of English: Crystal, ‘Why a Global Language?’, from *English as a Global Language* (Cambridge University Press, 2003), pp.1-27.

This week looks back to some of the important histories of how English became a global language, particularly the connections with capitalism and colonialism. It will explore the charge that English today is a form of linguistic imperialism.

Mistakes and Creativity: Quirk, ‘Language varieties and standard language’, *English Today* 21 (1990), pp.3-10.

What do people look for when learning a language? This week wonders why, if we would never imagine World Russians or World Chineses, it seems important to accept World Englishes. We will ask if English still has clearly defined centres of authority, or whether it is now dispersed and plural.

Standards: Modiano, ‘International English in the Global Village’, *English Today* 58 (1999), pp.22-27.

This week’s discussion will focus on the need for standards, and question whether our standards should come from native speakers. Many aspects of international life (scientific discourse, aviation) currently depend on English, but perhaps native speakers are not well placed to help develop these standards. We will think about why that might be true, and how we should test Englishes in future.

English in Asia: Kachru, ‘English as an Asian Language’, *Links and Letters* 5 (1998), pp.89-108.

The focus of the course shifts this week to Asia as a specific example (or series of examples) of the World Englishes. In some ways, English does not appear to belong in Asia, and yet it has clearly been accepted, and we will explore the different ways English functions and has penetrated cultural life.

Singapore: Ho, ‘“I’m not west. I’m not east. So how leh?”’, *English Today* 87 (2006), pp.17-24.

Singapore allows us to think about the general issues clearly because it is a restricted example. Everyone is familiar with Singlish, but what exactly does Singlish show us about the cultural life and social development of this relatively young city-state? The week examines different and sometimes contradictory aspects of English in Singapore.

Japan: Stanlaw, ‘The dynamics of English words in contemporary Japanese’, from *Japan English* (Hong Kong University Press, 2004), pp.11-37.

Shifting attention from former British colonies, this week explores Japan as an example where English seems to depend on another native speaker country, the United States. However, if we ask how Japanese society and culture actually uses English, far from being dependent on native speaker standards, we see independent creativity.

China: Hu, ‘Why China English should stand alongside British, American, and the other “World Englishes”’, *English Today* 78 (2004), pp.26-33.

Given the rise of China, it is obvious to ask, does China even need English? And does it need a specific China English? Exploring varied examples, this week considers the long history of English in China and some possible futures, in particular of Putonghua alongside English.

courses.subject: ENGE
courses.title: Acquisition of English As a Second Language
courses.description: This course is developed to introduce key concepts in learning English as a second language (L2) from a range of theoretical perspectives such as linguistic approach (e.g., universal grammar), interactionist approach (e.g., the Interaction hypothesis), frequency-based approach (e.g., connectionism) and sociocultural approach (e.g., constructivism). In addition, this course reviews the role of individual differences in L2 English learning such as learners’ first language, the onset age of L2 English learning, language learning aptitude, and motivation. This course aims to lead students to understand the relationship between theory and research, and thereby generate pedagogical implications to English education in the Chinese/Asian contexts.
courses.learning_outcomes:
After completing this course, students should:
• Gain a basic understanding of SLA concepts and theories
• Be able to discuss SLA research findings intelligently
• Be able to read SLA research articles critically
• Develop an understanding of and an interest in SLA research

courses.subject: ENGE
courses.title: Language and Society
courses.description: This course serves as a general introduction to the field of sociolinguistics – the study of the relationship between language and society. Students will be introduced to basic theories, research methods, and analytical tools in sociolinguistics. They will also be given opportunities to investigate the link between language practices and social factors (e.g., ethnicity, gender, and class). Topics covered in this course include code-switching, language variation and change, language in the media, styles, identity, language attitudes, as well as language policy. Examples will be drawn from a wide range of social contexts, with a focus on the English language. Sociolinguistic topics in Hong Kong are given particular consideration.
courses.learning_outcomes:
By the end of the course, students are expected to

1. be familiar with central concepts for and approaches to analyzing language in social context,
2. be able to engage with theoretical discussions in sociolinguistics and apply them to everyday sociolinguistic phenomena,
3. know how to carry out basic analyses of sociolinguistic patterns, and
4. have developed a capacity to critically reflect on issues in language and society that they encounter in daily life.

courses.subject: ENGE
courses.title: Drama in Performance II
courses.description: This course follows on from Drama in Performance I and will follow a similar pattern: students will study short plays and excerpts from longer plays, workshop them in small groups, with the aim of performing pieces of similar length before an audience at the end of the course. The two plays selected for Drama in Performance II have been deliberately chosen to reflect non-realist, non-naturalistic traditions in Western Theatre. By the end of the course, students would have learned how to stage abstract theatre, paying particular attention to physical movement and the delivery of poetry.
courses.learning_outcomes:
Students will learn:

to analyze dramatic texts as performance texts.

to create and perform a character on stage.

to use their voice and body to communicate to an audience.

to utilise theoretical understanding of a dramatic text in performance.

to articulate English more effectively.
courses.course_syllabus:
Performance Skills

Voice Production (intonation, pronunciation, clarity, projection); movement and gesture; communicating to an audience.

Theatrical Techniques

Creating a character through voice and movement; conveying feelings and thoughts through words and gestures.

Dramatic Form

Different dramatic styles and forms (e.g. a Radio play, Shakespearen comedy); the nature of dialogue; dramatic theory; the stage

End of course evaluation questionnaire, staff self-evaluation, staff performance review

External Examiner

Periodic course review in normal departmental planning and review cycle

courses.subject: ENGE
courses.title: Language and Intercultural Communication
courses.description: This course aims to help students enhance their communication with people who have a different language and cultural background. Topics include the concept of culture; language and cultural socialization; cultural variations in verbal and nonverbal communication; language, identity and biases; cultural diversity in perception, values, and worldview; intercultural-intimate relationships; culture shock and intercultural adjustment/adaptation; intercultural conflict and mediation; and English in the global workplace. This course should be of interest to local and international students, and is especially appropriate for those who plan to travel, study, or work abroad.
courses.course_syllabus:
1. Introduction to course/ Defining communication and culture

2. The dimensions of intercultural communication

3. Cultural patterns and communication: beliefs, values, and worldview

4. Cultural, linguistic and ethnic identities, cultural biases and intercultural contact

5. Intercultural adjustment/ adaptation: culture shock and recovery

6. Verbal interaction communication across cultures

7. Cultural variations in persuasion/ the structure of conversations

8. Nonverbal communication across cultures (body language, time and space)

9. Constructive intercultural conflict management

10. Intercultural competence in interpersonal relationships

11. The development of intercultural communication competence & intercultural personhood

courses.subject: ENGE
courses.title: Pedagogical Grammar of English
courses.description: The aim of this course is to equip students with the grammatical knowledge that is essential for ESL teaching and learning. The first part of the course introduces students to key concepts involved in grammar pedagogy and teaching methodologies. The second part of the course covers grammatical forms and structures in English that may be particularly difficult for second language learners to acquire, with an emphasis on understanding the connection between form and meaning. Students apply what they have learned through conducting mini-lessons and team-teaching. At the end of the course, students should feel more confident about the use of grammar terminology in second language teaching and more knowledgeable about a task-based and discourse-oriented approach to grammar teaching in second language classrooms.
courses.enrollment_requirement: Prerequisite: ENGE1520 (only applicable to the 334 new curriculum)
courses.learning_outcomes:
After completing this course, students should:

‧ understand basic concepts in grammar pedagogy

‧ appreciate the relation between form, meaning and use in English grammar

‧ design grammar teaching materials that demonstrate understanding of grammar usage

‧ become more aware of the discourse functions of grammar forms in various text types

‧ become more aware of issues involved in the teaching and learning of English grammar
courses.course_syllabus: The aim of the course is to equip students with the grammatical knowledge that is essential for ESL teaching and learning. The first part of the course will introduce students to key concepts involved in grammar pedagogy and teaching methodologies. The second part of the course will cover grammatical forms and structures in English that may be particularly difficult for second language learners to acquire, with an emphasis on understanding the connection between form and meaning. Students will apply what they have learned through conducting mini-lessons and team-teaching. At the end of the course, students should become more knowledgeable about the contextual use of grammar and more articulate in the grammar terminology commonly used in teaching and learning.

courses.subject: ENGE
courses.title: Lexical Studies in English
courses.description: This course introduces students to lexicology, the branch of linguistics concerned with the study of the vocabulary of a given language. Focusing on but not limited to English, this course aims at developing students’ sensitivity to the English lexicon and enabling them to apply linguistic principles in morphology, syntax, and semantics to the systematic description of English words. The course covers the notion of word meaning, the various lexical relations among words, the historical origin and development of the English vocabulary, the major word-formation processes in English, the nature and pervasiveness of figurative language, and ways English words are used in particular contexts.
courses.course_syllabus:
Part One: Words in Language

lexicology; history of English words

Part Two: Words in Linguistic Analysis

morphological, phonological and semantic analyses of words

Part Three: Words in Use

language variation; lexical change; collocations; MWUs

Part Four: Words in L2 Teaching and Learning

vocabulary acquisition; metaphor and metonymy in figurative language

courses.subject: ENGE
courses.title: Communications for English Majors III

meetings.location: William M W Mong Eng Bldg 406

courses.description: The aim of the course is to strengthen students’ writing and speaking skills for a wide range of communicative contexts. Students will take advantage of different writing tasks to polish their descriptive, narrative and analytical writing skills. They will also learn to appreciate and apply different tones and styles for different workplace writing purposes. Through working on a feature report, students will also become more competent in research, analysis and the organization and presentation of ideas. In addition, students are encouraged to capitalize on the small-class environment by taking a proactive role in class discussions and presentations. The job interview and panel discussion exercises also provide students with opportunities to enhance their oral skills. It is hoped that by the end of the course, students can become competent and confident users of English across a variety of contexts.
courses.enrollment_requirement: Prerequisites: ENGE1310 and 1320.
courses.learning_outcomes:
After completing this course, students should be able to:
- Write more clearly, concisely and coherently, and speak with more confidence
- Adopt the appropriate diction, tone and style for different communicative contexts
- Produce professional workplace correspondences and perform in job interviews
- Conduct basic research on a chosen topic and develop intelligent ideas and opinions
- Express opinions and discuss ideas critically in a panel discussion setting
courses.course_syllabus:
1. Writing across different contexts
a) Through a series of very short writing exercises, you warm up your writing muscles and explore different types of writing. The aim is to help you understand the elements of good writing and also refine your choice of words and sentence structures.
b) discussing and writing about current affairs, you will learn to summarize, evaluate and persuade. Please take this opportunity to form the habit of reading international newspapers, magazines and journals.

2. Workplace English
First, get the job by preparing professional resumes and honing your job interview skills. Then do your job by writing proper business letters and workplace correspondences for different purposes.

3. Feature Report + Panel Discussion
What are some issues that you and your peers care about? (What makes you angry, frustrated, motivated, hopeful?) What are some phenomena that will shape your future? How can you find out more on these topics and, more important, form your opinion? You begin with a general topic and proceed to do research and analysis, and learn how to investigate and research issues intelligently, write a report that is informative and critical, and also prepare a panel discussion to orally present the gist of your work.
courses.feedback_evaluation: Students will be asked to complete Course Evaluations for ENGE 2330 at the end of the semester. Comments and suggestions about the course content, teaching method and learning tasks are most welcome at any time during the semester.
courses.required_readings: Learning materials will be distributed in class. Assigned readings should be done before class. You are strongly encouraged to form the habit of reading the news in English on a daily basis.

courses.subject: ENGE
courses.title: Romanticism
courses.description: This course will address itself to the origin of Romanticism, its development and application as a conceptual model; it will involve the study of romantic poetry in the English tradition, with reference to some major themes such as nature, time, love and death.
courses.course_syllabus:
Romanticism

~ the complexity and multiplicity of the term “Romanticism”

~ Romanticism in English poetic tradition, its relation to Neo-classicism and Modernism

~ British Romanticism and European Romanticism

~ tensions in Romantic poetry:

- the role of imagination and of experience

- Romantic self and poetic persona

- self-expressiveness and sympathy

- radicalism and escapism

- the meaning of “nature”

- the supernatural, the exotic, and the gothic

courses.subject: ENGE
courses.title: Literature and Film
courses.description:
(UGED3143 is double-coded with ENGE3220.)

The aim of this course is to familiarize students with the kindred relationships between film and literature as well as their essential mediumistic differences such as film and the novel, film and drama/theatre, film and poetry, etc. Their similar or different uses of time and space, and problems of adaptation will be discussed in some works which have both filmic and literary versions.
courses.learning_outcomes:
It is hoped that after taking this course students will be able to:

Analyze film narratives and compare them to their literary counterparts.

Have a better understanding of how different film techniques contribute to creating meaning in visual media.

Apply their knowledge to the films they may watch outside the classroom as well as be aware of the literary sources from which films might be adaptated.

courses.subject: ENGE
courses.title: Writing a Life Between Languages
courses.description: This course aims first to introduce students to autobiography, one of the most popular forms of writing in the contemporary world. It will raise such questions as, why has this kind of narrative been so attractive to writers in cross-cultural situations, such as Chinese-Americans? The course will focus specifically on a group of (mainly American) immigrant autobiographers who write about the experience of living between a first language and English. They raise a range of fascinating questions about language and identity. Students will be asked to reflect on these questions and on the similarities and differences between these writers’ lives and their own experience in Hong Kong. The course will aim to teach students how to approach, analyse and theorize about autobiographical narratives. Students will be given opportunity to develop their analytical skills in essay-type responses to the prescribed texts. They will also be given opportunity to develop their narrative skills in their own life-writing, using the prescribed texts as models. Both forms of written exercise are designed to extend, as well as test, students’ understanding of autobiography as a genre.
courses.learning_outcomes:
• To teach students how to approach, analyze and theorize about autobiographical narratives.

• To develop analytical skills in essay-type responses to prescribed texts.

• To develop their narrative skills in their own life writing, using the prescribed texts as models.

• To develop and test student understanding of autobiography as genre.

courses.subject: ENGE
courses.title: Reading and Writing Short Stories
courses.description: This course follows ENGE3260 but is not dependent on it. It provides an introduction to the craft of writing short stories. Students will read and analyse a range of short stories of diverse styles and forms from the beginnings of the genre to the present day. Particular attention will be given to contemporary stories written in Hong Kong. Students will be encouraged to use these texts as models for their own creative responses. The course aims both to deepen and broaden students’ understanding of the short story genre as well as to offer them the opportunity of gaining practical expertise in creative writing.
courses.learning_outcomes:
1. Students learn to recognize the part played by various features of a short story such as point of view, narration, shape, beginnings and ends, focus and narrative voice.

2. Students learn to recognize various styles of short story such as realist, modernist (mosaic style), metafictional.

3. Students come to understand how certain strategies tend to enhance the quality of a story, such as “showing” versus “telling”, or planting a seed of meaning at the beginning of a story.

4. Students learn to recognize the salient strengths and weaknesses in their work and those in the work of others.

5. They learn how to give constructive advice to their peers on how to improve their writing.

6. Students learn how to write a creative response to a story they have read by changing the point of view, for example, or imitating its style.

7. Students learn how to plan a complete original story, write it, respond to feedback and revise it.
courses.course_syllabus:
Point of view and focalization

Students learn the importance of adopting a consistent point of view

Setting and dialogue

Students learn about “showing” versus “telling”

Shaping a story

Students learn about having a clear focus

Beginnings and ends

Students learn that these are important in shaping a story.

Characterization

Students learn how to build characters around focal ideas

Planning and plotting

Students learn about writing as an act of discovery

Revising

Students learn about the importance of drafting and redrafting

courses.subject: ENGE
courses.title: Hong Kong Literature in English
courses.description: Students will study Hong Kong literature in English in its colonial and postcolonial contexts. Works from different genres will be discussed to explore the following issues: the representation of Hong Kong and its people, the question of identity, the agency of its people, the impact of modernity and postmodernity, and the ideas of home and exile. Students’ analytical and critical skills will be sharpened to enhance their understanding of literary works, their awareness of the dynamic interrelationships between literature and social-political forces, and their appreciation of the meeting points between Hong Kong literature in English and Hong Kong literature in Chinese.
courses.learning_outcomes: By the end of the course, students should be able to have acquired an acquaintance with the basic concepts and methodology of comparative literary study. They should be able to critique notions of modernization and modernity and problematize the terms East and West. They will have acquired knowledge of how certain fiction writers and poets in the twentieth century learned from cultures and literatures other than their own.

courses.description: This course is designed to investigate topics in creative writing not normally covered under generic creative writing courses. It may involve any one or more of the following areas in creative writing studies: literary community and global citizenship, writing for new media, creative writing and other arts, creative writing and social enterprises, phenomenology and creative writing, creative writing and research, writing speculative fiction, writing flash fiction, teaching creative writing, history of creative writing, and the writing of auto-ethnography. Students are allowed to take the above course more than once and gain the units each time they pass the course. However, students cannot take courses with the same course code more than once in a single term.

courses.subject: ENGE
courses.title: Writing Hong Kong
courses.description: This course focuses on the art of writing about Hong Kong. Through the use of writing prompts, it introduces students to the different ways of writing about different social and physical environments in Hong Kong. Students will be able to discuss and articulate the feelings, thoughts and experiences evoked by these social and physical environments. They will be able to produce writings that evoke the sense of place that is unique to Hong Kong. They will be able to consider issues such as genre, gender and language use in relation to readership. Through active participation in class discussion and exploration of actual environments, students will acquire different perspectives and understand the diverse possibilities in relation to writing about Hong Kong. They will be able to assess their own writing and those of others.
courses.learning_outcomes:
By the end of this course a student will have:

i) A greater understanding of what it means to write about Hong Kong in English.

ii) Considered the various genres and their diverse possibilities in relation to writing about Hong Kong.

iii) Experience in assessing their writing as well as those of others.

iv) Acquired and discussed techniques of writing about Hong Kong.

courses.subject: ENGE
courses.title: Ekphrasis: Encounters between Verbal and Visual Arts
courses.description: This course will study literature that represents visual arts or, ekphrasis. The term ‘ekphrasis’ can be broadly defined as verbal representation of visual arts and has become a vibrant field of study for a few decades. We will look at some most famous ekphrastic texts from the beginning of Western literature to this day, focusing on poems evoking either actual or imaginary artworks, but branching out to ekphrasis in fiction and new media as well. In the process, we will reflect on the evolution of the term ‘ekphrasis’ from a rhetorical device to a site of rivalry and on to a mode of encounter between the verbal and visual medium. The course will explore interactions of multiple media while foregrounding characteristics of each medium.

courses.subject: ENGE
courses.title: Reading Alice Munro and Writing Life-Stories
courses.description: In this course, we will read a selection of Alice Munro’s short stories and analyze them as the author’s retrospection, reconfiguration and reimagining of places, people and events in her own life. From the recasting of her mother, father and other family members in her works to the reconstruction of tales of tragedy, love and faith, Munro writes in order to open up the past, take a good look at it, feel the life, and discover the story. This idea of life as story and the power of storying lives in the construction self and identity is drawn from narrative psychology and will be used as a springboard for students to experiment with life-writing in the course. Students will be given opportunities to re-tell the stories of their own lives and/or the lives of their family and friends in ways that allow a re-interpretation of the past as well as a re-orientation of the future.
courses.learning_outcomes:
After completing this course, students should:

* Become familiar with the works of Alice Munro and be able to appreciate her artistry
* Becomes familiar with the elements of short fiction and life narratives
* Be able to analyze short stories in terms of themes and techniques
* Understand the storied nature of human lives and the power of life-writing
* Have assembled a portfolio of life-writing works
* Have produced a complete life-story for workshopping
* Have acquired the analytical skills to participate constructively in workshops
courses.course_syllabus:
| **Topic** | **Contents/fundamental concepts** |
| --- | --- |
| Storying and (re)storying our lives | The narrative structure of human lives Self and narrative identity How to conduct narrative analysis Eudaimonic well-being and redemptive life narratives |
| Alice Munro’s “approach and recognize” method | The “glorious leap” The “marvelous clear jelly” that is art |
| Writing life stories | Life chapters, childhood memories, peak/nadir event, photo story, a symbolic message, an enigma, turning points, the future script |
| Reading Alice Munro | Themes and techniques, criss-crossing of experience and reflection, layering of time, use of flashbacks, narrative arcs |

courses.subject: ENGE
courses.title: Contrastive Linguistics
courses.description: This course looks at how comparisons can be made between languages with respect to their sound systems, lexical-semantic structures, sentence patterns, and pragmatic properties. Through systematic analysis, students gain understanding from research findings in language typology, language universals, and language acquisition. This course focuses on the basic characteristics of English and Chinese, and some salient contrasts between them. The approach is largely descriptive without assuming prior knowledge of theoretical syntax. The course also considers how comparisons between languages may help students associated with problems of translation and language teaching/learning.
courses.learning_outcomes:
After completing this course, students should be able to:

- conduct contrastive analyses of English and Chinese

- understand the theories in contrastive linguistics

- apply contrastive linguistics to language learning and teaching

courses.subject: ENGE
courses.title: Psycholinguistics
courses.description: This course introduces psycholinguistics as it relates to language processing, acquisition, and development. We survey a range of topics such as language and the brain; real-time language processing and associated neuroimaging research; learning and memory; learner characteristics, age effects, and the critical period hypothesis; bi- and multi-lingualism; abnormal language development, language breakdown, and dyslexia; animal communication systems; which in turn shed light on mechanisms supporting human language and cognition.
courses.enrollment_requirement: Not for students who have taken PSYC3430

~ regarding the difference between language description and language use

~ regarding the structure of the brain

~ regarding the major areas of language performance

~ regarding the relationship between language, thought, and context

- to develop your ability to write cogently and analytically on theoretical and empirical material, and also to write effectively in relation to data you have collected.

- in class, to develop your ability to participate effectively in discussion

- to develop your ability to read the literature on psycholinguistics, and make presentations about it which are clear, concise, and helpful to other students, as well as fair representations of any data involved

courses.subject: ENGE
courses.title: Language, Meaning, and Text
courses.description: This course introduces some major approaches as well as basic principles and tools in the analysis of texts. Concepts such as cohesion and coherence, relevance, context, and identity are drawn upon to analyze a range of discourses and text types in our contemporary society. Texts to be discussed include advertisements, news reports, interviews, political speeches, websites, book reviews, etc. The relevance of discourse analysis to foreign/second language teaching may also be considered.
courses.course_syllabus:
English 3670 is an introductory course on some fundamental issues in analyzing written and spoken texts. Given that
discourse analysis is concerned with the systematic and careful examination of a variety of texts, this course will be
organized according to the various ways linguists may interpret the notion of text.
This semester, we begin with a general review of discourse analysis as a field of linguistic study. Then, we follow that
discussion with an overview of various rhetorical and linguistic approaches to the analysis of texts as written discourse,
examining non-fiction (essays) and literary texts. Our stylistic analyses of writing includes the study of rhetorical aspects
(aims, purpose, rhetorical situation, style, discourse blocs, modes of development, voice/persona, tone, attitude, and
readability) and syntactic matters (i.e., lexical, phrasal, and clausal use). With an understanding of this approach, we use it to
analyze student-written essays; in addition, we consider what our findings may suggest about the texts and their writers,
with a view to identifying strategies for possible rewriting and revising.
Moving from text as written discourse, we adopt a broad notion of the term text as both written and spoken discourse. Primarily
using the work of text-linguists, we consider structures “beyond the sentence.” That is, we now view the idea of a text as
any passage, either spoken or written, of whatever length, that forms a unified whole; and we consider what makes a text
“good” or “successful” in terms of information flow (the way ideas are dispersed throughout a text) , coherence (global
concern that indicates a text is unified), and cohesion (local or surface concern that serve to link one idea and sentence to
the next one in a text). Again, once we complete some cohesion and information flow analyses, we look at practical ways
we might apply some of the key ideas in teaching and learning situations in Hong Kong.
Finally, we conclude with the notion of text as talking—oral exchanges. We will analyze naturally-occurring language in use, i.e.,
conversational analysis and pragmatics. To do so, we will cover such concepts such as the Gricean maxims and the
Cooperative Principle (rules and conditions which speakers abide by in effective or “happy” exchanges), implicature
(indirect meaning), speech acts (social act performed by the uttering of certain words), as well as associated phenomenon
(e.g., framing, turn-taking, overlap, interruption, adjacency pairs, etc. ). Our study of text as talk will enable us to explore
how and why people “do” things with words, sometimes successfully and sometimes not.
By the end of the course, we hope to have a better understanding of how discourse analysis reveals to us the intricacies of a
text as well as enhances our knowledge of how such analysis can strengthen our abilities as language users—speakers and
writers.

courses.description: This course provides an introduction to the relationship between gender and language use. Drawing from empirical and theoretical studies in sociolinguistics, psycholinguistics, and discourse analysis, this course addresses a range of issues. Examples of key issues include the acquisition of gender-differentiated language, gender and conversational interaction (e.g. extralinguistic constructions of gender or politeness, hedging or interruptions), sexism in language, gender images, and the relationships among language, gender, and other social constructs such as class, culture, and ethnicity.
courses.course_syllabus:
Gender & Language
Stereotypes about Wo/men’s Language
Social and Linguistic Changes in Wo/men’s Speech
Gender Differentiation, Quantitative Sociolinguistics, and Social Networks
Conversational Interaction
Development of Gender-Appropriate Speech
Gender and Language Change
Sexism in Language Use
